title: Crash resume preserves attempt counter across SIGKILL
description: When a process is killed mid-step and then resume() is called, the attempt counter (state.steps[id].attempts) must carry forward from the crashed run — it must not be reset to 0 or capped to 1. The existing crash-resume.test.ts verifies that zombie steps are re-run, but does not assert that the attempts counter is incremented from its pre-crash value. This is the foundation of the retry budget contract: each crash counts as an attempt, and the budget is cumulative across crashes.
input: Seed a run directory with state.json: step-A (status:'succeeded', attempts:1), step-B (status:'running', attempts:1). Write a valid flow-ref.json pointing to a two-step linear flow (step-A -> step-B). Call resume() with a MockProvider that makes step-B succeed on its next attempt.
expected_output: resume() completes. After the zombie sweep, step-B is transitioned to 'pending' with attempts still at 1. When dispatched, the state machine increments it to 2 before execution. Final state.json shows step-B with attempts:2 and status:'succeeded'. MockProvider.invoke() is called exactly once.

title: Partial parallel resume skips already-succeeded branches
description: A parallel step fans out to branches A and B. If branch-A succeeds but branch-B fails in the original run, the parallel parent is marked failed. On resume(), the parallel step retries. Branch-A must NOT be re-dispatched — the executeParallel logic checks getBranchStatus() before dispatch and short-circuits. This is the core idempotency contract for parallel execution under partial failure. Without it, already-billed prompt steps would be re-billed. Verify this is not already covered by integration/resume-complex-races.test.ts before implementing.
input: Flow with: parallel step (branches:['branchA','branchB']), branchA (prompt step), branchB (prompt step). Seed state.json: parallel=failed (attempts:1), branchA=succeeded (attempts:1), branchB=failed (attempts:1). Write matching flow-ref.json. Call resume() with MockProvider where branchB returns success.
expected_output: MockProvider.invoke() is called exactly once total (for branchB, not branchA). Final state: parallel=succeeded, branchA=succeeded (attempts:1 unchanged), branchB=succeeded (attempts:2). run() resolves without error.

title: onFail:'continue' allows dependent steps to execute after upstream failure
description: When a step fails and its onFail policy is 'continue', the DAG walker must mark the step as 'failed' but still enqueue all steps that have it in their dependsOn. This enables optional best-effort steps that do not block downstream processing. The dependent step must execute even though its predecessor failed. Without this, a non-critical step failure would halt an entire pipeline.
input: Flow with step-A (prompt step, onFail:'continue', no dependsOn), step-B (prompt step, dependsOn:['step-A']). MockProvider configured to always fail step-A with a generic Error and to succeed step-B. Call run() with this flow and MockProvider.
expected_output: run() resolves without throwing. Final state: step-A status='failed', step-B status='succeeded'. The run result does not short-circuit at step-A. MockProvider.invoke() called twice (once for each step). Overall run status reflects step-A failed but run completed.

title: onFail:<stepId> routes execution to recovery step, bypasses normal dependents
description: When a step fails and its onFail names another step ID (not 'abort' or 'continue'), the DAG walker must enqueue that specific step and NOT enqueue steps that normally depend on the failed step. This enables recovery workflows (fail -> cleanup -> done). The recovery step executes even though it is not in the normal dependency chain. Steps that normally follow the failed step must be skipped entirely.
input: Flow with three steps: step-A (prompt, onFail:'cleanup'), step-B (prompt, dependsOn:['step-A']), step-cleanup (prompt, standalone — no dependsOn, not in normal topology from step-A). MockProvider fails step-A and succeeds step-cleanup. Call run().
expected_output: step-cleanup executes and succeeds. step-B does NOT execute (MockProvider.invoke() never called for step-B). step-A status='failed'. step-cleanup status='succeeded'. run() resolves without throwing.

title: State version mismatch blocks resume
description: If a user bumps their flow version between runs, resuming an old run with the new flow definition risks schema mismatch, wrong step IDs, and corrupted handoff data. The resume() function must compare the flowName and flowVersion in state.json against the loaded Flow object and reject the resume if either differs. This is the guard that prevents silent data corruption from flow evolution.
input: Create a run directory with state.json containing runId, flowName:'my-flow', flowVersion:'0.1.0', status:'failed', and one step in failed state. Write flow-ref.json pointing to a flow module. Load a Flow object with name:'my-flow' and version:'0.2.0'. Call resume() with this mismatched flow.
expected_output: resume() throws (or returns err with) StateVersionMismatchError. error.code is 'relay_STATE_VERSION_MISMATCH'. No MockProvider.invoke() calls are made. No step state transitions occur.

title: Retry budget enforced cumulatively across crash and resume
description: A step with maxRetries:1 is allowed at most 2 total attempts (1 original + 1 retry). If state.json already records attempts:2 for a failed step, then resume() must not grant any additional attempts. The retry budget is cumulative across the original run and all resumes. This prevents infinite retry loops when a flow is resumed repeatedly after persistent failures.
input: Flow with a single prompt step (maxRetries:1). Seed state.json with that step: status:'failed', attempts:2 (budget fully consumed in original run). Call resume() with a MockProvider that always fails.
expected_output: The step is recognized as having exhausted its retry budget. Either: (a) MockProvider.invoke() is not called at all and the step immediately remains failed, or (b) it is called once and fails without retry (no backoff delay, no second attempt). Final run status:'failed'. No additional MockProvider calls beyond 1.

title: HandoffSchemaError carries Zod validation issues, not just a message
description: When a prompt step produces JSON output that fails Zod schema validation, the resulting HandoffSchemaError must carry the structured Zod issues in its details. This enables downstream tooling (CLI error formatter, relay logs) to surface exactly which fields failed and why — not just 'schema validation failed'. The error.details.issues must be the parsed array of Zod $ZodIssue objects including path and message for each violation.
input: Instantiate a HandoffStore pointing to a temp directory. Call store.write('myHandoff', { wrong: 'shape', extra: 123 }, z.object({ correct: z.string(), count: z.number() })). Await the result.
expected_output: Returns err(HandoffSchemaError). error.code === 'relay_HANDOFF_SCHEMA'. error.details.issues is a non-empty array. Each element has at minimum a 'path' property (array) and a 'message' property (string) identifying the failed field. The issues array must contain at least two entries: one for missing 'correct' and one for missing 'count' (or extra properties depending on strictness).

title: AtomicWrite leaves original file intact when rename fails
description: The atomicWrite function writes content to a temp file then renames it to the target. If the rename fails (e.g., ENOSPC, cross-device link), the original target must be completely untouched. This is the core durability guarantee for state.json, handoffs, and metrics. A failed rename that leaves the target file corrupted or empty would make recovery impossible. Verify this is not already tested in util/atomic-write.test.ts for the ENOSPC case before implementing.
input: Create a target file containing the string 'original content'. Mock fs.promises.rename to throw an Error with code 'ENOSPC'. Call atomicWrite(targetPath, Buffer.from('new content')). Catch the returned error.
expected_output: atomicWrite returns err(AtomicWriteError) with code 'relay_ATOMIC_WRITE'. Reading the target file yields 'original content' (byte-for-byte identical to before the call). The temp file (which will have a different name) either does not exist or exists as an orphan — the target is never touched.

title: Lint: all five required README sections trigger errors when missing
description: The flow package linter checks for five required README sections per §7.4: 'What it does', 'Sample output', 'Estimated cost and duration', 'Install', 'Run'. If any is missing, a LintFinding error must be reported. This is the only check preventing undocumented flows from being published to the catalog. Test that each section is individually detected — a README with exactly four of five sections should produce exactly one error. No existing lint test file exists.
input: Create a temp flow directory with a valid package.json (name, version, type:module, main, relay block). Write a README.md containing the headings for 'What it does', 'Estimated cost and duration', 'Install', and 'Run' — but omitting 'Sample output'. Call lintFlowPackage(dir).
expected_output: Returns ok(LintReport). report.errors.length >= 1. At least one finding has a code or message referencing 'Sample output' or 'sample_output'. report.errors does NOT contain findings for the four sections that ARE present.

title: Lint: missing prompts/ directory flagged when step uses promptFile
description: If a flow's package.json (or compiled flow) declares a step with a promptFile reference, the linter must verify that the prompts/ directory exists in the package. A missing prompts/ directory means the flow cannot run — the prompt files were not included in the npm package. This catches a common packaging mistake before publish. No existing lint test file exists.
input: Create a temp flow directory with a package.json whose relay block (or entry-point duck-type) implies a step using promptFile:'prompts/01_step.md'. Do NOT create a prompts/ directory. Include a valid README with all required sections. Call lintFlowPackage(dir).
expected_output: Returns ok(LintReport). report.errors contains at least one finding whose message or code references the missing prompts directory or the missing promptFile. The finding must be categorized as an error (not just a warning).

title: Lint: malformed relay metadata block fields produce targeted errors
description: The relay block in package.json must contain estimatedCostUsd as {min:number, max:number}, estimatedDurationMin as {min:number, max:number}, tags as string[], and audience as string[]. Providing a flat number instead of a range object (e.g., estimatedCostUsd: 0.5) must produce a LintFinding error that names the offending field. Test multiple malformed shapes to confirm field-level granularity. No existing lint test file exists.
input: Three sub-cases: (1) package.json with relay.estimatedCostUsd: 0.5 (flat number). (2) package.json with relay.tags: 'not-an-array' (string instead of array). (3) package.json with relay block missing the 'displayName' field entirely. Call lintFlowPackage(dir) for each.
expected_output: Each case returns ok(LintReport) with report.errors containing at least one finding. The finding for case 1 references estimatedCostUsd. The finding for case 2 references tags. The finding for case 3 references displayName or a required-field message. No false positives for correct fields.

title: Input parser assigns positional argv to required schema fields in declaration order
description: When a user runs `relay run my-flow Alice Wonderland`, the two positional arguments must be assigned to the flow's required input fields in the order they are declared in the Zod schema. This is the primary ergonomic UX for simple single-or-double-argument flows. The assignment must be positional (declaration order in the schema), not alphabetical.
input: Schema: z.object({ firstName: z.string().describe('First name'), lastName: z.string().describe('Last name') }). Argv (the extra args after the command): ['Alice', 'Wonderland'] (no named flags). Call parseInput(schema, argv).
expected_output: Returns ok({ firstName: 'Alice', lastName: 'Wonderland' }). firstName gets 'Alice' (first positional = first declared required field). lastName gets 'Wonderland' (second positional = second declared required field). No validation errors.

title: Input parser: named --key=value flags take precedence over positional assignment
description: When a user mixes named flags and positional arguments, named flags must win. The flag --firstName=Alice explicitly names the field; any remaining positional args are assigned to the remaining unset required fields. If both a named flag and a positional would target the same field, the named flag value is authoritative.
input: Schema: z.object({ firstName: z.string(), lastName: z.string() }). Argv: ['--firstName=Alice', 'Wonderland']. Call parseInput(schema, argv).
expected_output: Returns ok({ firstName: 'Alice', lastName: 'Wonderland' }). firstName comes from the --firstName=Alice flag. lastName comes from the remaining positional 'Wonderland'. No conflict error.

title: Exit code mapping: all PipelineError subclasses map to distinct non-zero codes
description: The exit-codes.ts module is the contract between Relay and shell script consumers. Every distinct error category must have a unique non-zero exit code so scripts can branch on specific failure types. If two error classes share a code, or an error class maps to 0, shell scripts will misroute failures. This test validates the complete mapping table is internally consistent.
input: Instantiate one instance of each of these error classes with minimal required args: StepFailureError, FlowDefinitionError, ClaudeAuthError, HandoffSchemaError, NoProviderConfiguredError, TimeoutError, AuthTimeoutError, ProviderRateLimitError, AtomicWriteError. Pass each to the exit-code resolver function (getExitCode or equivalent in exit-codes.ts).
expected_output: Each error maps to a non-zero integer in the range 1-8. The set of return values has no duplicates — all 9 inputs produce 9 distinct codes. A plain Error (not a PipelineError) maps to 1. The SIGINT/paused case (not an error instance) maps to 130.

title: Flow loader resolves flows in priority order: path > .relay/flows > node_modules
description: The flow loader must respect a strict resolution order so that a local override (./my-flow) always beats a cached installed version. An ambiguous name that could be an installed flow or a node_modules package must resolve from .relay/flows first. This order must be deterministic and not rely on filesystem coincidence. A missing-at-all-locations name must produce a FlowLoadError with a 'relay install' remediation hint.
input: Three scenarios using a temp project structure: (1) arg is './my-flow' and a valid flow package exists at that relative path — verify import from the explicit path; (2) arg is 'my-flow' (no slash prefix), .relay/flows/my-flow/ exists with a valid package — verify .relay/flows resolution; (3) arg is 'my-flow', .relay/flows absent, node_modules/@ganderbite/flow-my-flow/ exists — verify node_modules resolution. Fourth scenario: arg is 'no-such-flow', nothing found anywhere.
expected_output: Scenarios 1-3 each return ok(Flow) loaded from the expected location. Scenario 4 returns err(FlowLoadError) whose message includes 'relay install no-such-flow' or equivalent remediation text.

title: Flow loader duck-type guard rejects module missing required Flow properties
description: A flow package's default export must be a valid compiled Flow object. The loader performs a duck-type check because flow packages arrive as dynamic imports from potentially different runtime instances. It checks for: name (string), steps (object), graph (object with .get/.has methods for successors and predecessors), graph.topoOrder (array). An object missing graph.successors must fail the guard. This prevents silent crashes when a malformed or stub package is installed.
input: Mock the dynamic import mechanism to return a default export of: { name: 'test-flow', steps: {}, graph: {} } (graph present but missing successors/predecessors/topoOrder). Call loadFlow() with a path pointing to this mocked module.
expected_output: Returns err(FlowLoadError) with a code or message indicating the module is not a valid Flow (e.g., FLOW_INVALID or 'not a valid flow'). The error must not be a raw import error — it must be the duck-type rejection.

title: Config set: partial update preserves all unrelated settings keys
description: The `relay config set` command must read the existing settings.json, merge the single changed key, and write back the full object atomically. A naive full-overwrite with only the new key would erase all other settings the user has configured. This merge-and-write pattern is the core correctness contract for the config command.
input: Create a temp settings.json with: { "provider": "claude-cli", "telemetry": { "enabled": true }, "color": "always" }. Invoke the config set handler with key='provider', value='claude-api'.
expected_output: settings.json is updated to { "provider": "claude-api", "telemetry": { "enabled": true }, "color": "always" }. The telemetry and color keys are unchanged. The write is atomic (file contents valid JSON even if process is killed mid-write, i.e., uses temp-rename pattern).

title: Config set: unknown configuration key rejected, settings.json not modified
description: The relay settings schema only supports known keys (provider, telemetry.enabled, color). An attempt to set an unknown key like 'unknownSetting' or 'foo.bar' must be rejected with an error. settings.json must not be written at all — not even with the unknown key appended. This prevents typos from silently polluting the settings file with unrecognised fields.
input: Create a temp settings.json with: { "provider": "claude-cli" }. Invoke the config set handler with key='notARealKey', value='someValue'.
expected_output: Handler returns an error or exits with non-zero code. settings.json is byte-for-byte unchanged (still contains only { "provider": "claude-cli" }). The error message identifies the unknown key.

title: Registry generator partial success: valid entries returned when some inputs fail
description: generateRegistryJson accepts a mixed list of local paths and npm package names. When some inputs are invalid (path does not exist, npm package not found), the function must still return ok(RegistryDoc) with entries for the valid inputs. Only if ALL inputs fail should it return an error. This allows CI registry builds to succeed even when a new package hasn't been published yet.
input: Call generateRegistryJson(['./valid-flow-a', './does-not-exist', './valid-flow-b']) where valid-flow-a and valid-flow-b are temp directories with valid package.json (including relay block) and README.md files. ./does-not-exist does not exist on disk.
expected_output: Returns ok(RegistryDoc) where RegistryDoc.flows has exactly 2 entries corresponding to valid-flow-a and valid-flow-b. The failure for ./does-not-exist is either silently omitted or reported in a separate errors/warnings field. RegistryDoc.version === 1 and generatedAt is a valid ISO-8601 timestamp.

title: relay run exits code 6 with NoProviderConfiguredError when no provider configured at any tier
description: The three-tier provider resolution (flag > flow settings > global settings) must fail loudly with a specific error and exit code when none of the tiers supply a provider. Exit code 6 (NoProviderConfiguredError) is the contract for shell script consumers that check whether the user has run `relay init`. The error message must include actionable remediation (relay init or --provider flag). The existing run.test.ts only tests flag forwarding, not the missing-provider path.
input: Prepare a valid minimal flow (using MockProvider or a stub import). Invoke the run command handler with: no --provider flag, no .relay/settings.json in the flow directory, no ~/.relay/settings.json present (or stub the settings loaders to return null).
expected_output: The command handler exits with code 6 or returns/throws NoProviderConfiguredError. Stderr or stdout output contains a message referencing 'relay init' or '--provider' as remediation. No steps are executed.

title: First Ctrl-C during run triggers paused banner with exit 130, not failure banner
description: User interruption (SIGINT) must be distinguished from a run failure. The first Ctrl-C sets wasInterrupted=true and sends an abort signal to the orchestrator. When the orchestrator resolves with status='aborted', the CLI must render the paused banner (resumable state) rather than the failure banner. Exit code must be 130 (POSIX SIGINT convention). A second Ctrl-C within 2 seconds must hard-exit immediately.
input: Construct a multi-step flow where step-1 is slow (MockProvider delays before returning). Start the run. After step-1 is in progress, programmatically emit a SIGINT to the process (or call the SIGINT handler registered in run.ts). Let the orchestrator resolve.
expected_output: renderPausedBanner() is called (not renderFailureBanner()). Process exits with code 130. The paused output shows steps completed and steps cancelled. wasInterrupted is true at the point banner is selected.

title: defineFlow throws FlowDefinitionError for flow names violating kebab-case convention
description: Flow names are used as directory names, registry identifiers, and in state.json. They must be kebab-case (lowercase letters, digits, single hyphens between words, no leading/trailing hyphens). defineFlow must validate the name at load time and throw FlowDefinitionError rather than silently accepting invalid names that would cause downstream failures. This is a load-time authoring guard.
input: Four separate defineFlow calls with only the name changed (all other fields valid): (1) name:'MyFlow' (uppercase), (2) name:'my flow' (space), (3) name:'-myflow' (leading hyphen), (4) name:'my--flow' (double hyphen). Also test name:'my-flow-2' which IS valid.
expected_output: Cases 1-4 each throw FlowDefinitionError with code 'relay_FLOW_DEFINITION'. The error message identifies the offending name. Case 5 (my-flow-2) does NOT throw — it returns a valid Flow object.

title: Context injection renders all contextFrom handoffs as named template variables
description: When a prompt step specifies contextFrom:['handoff1','handoff2'], both handoff values must be injected into the Handlebars template before sending to Claude. The template variables {{handoff1.field}} and {{handoff2.field}} must both resolve independently. This tests the assemblePrompt / context injection logic in context-inject.ts. Verify this is not already tested there before implementing.
input: Create a HandoffStore (temp dir). Write handoff1 = { x: 42 } and handoff2 = { label: 'hello' }. Prepare a prompt template string: 'Value is {{handoff1.x}} and label is {{handoff2.label}}'. Call assemblePrompt (or the equivalent context-inject function) with contextFrom:['handoff1','handoff2'] and the template.
expected_output: Returned rendered string is exactly 'Value is 42 and label is hello'. No {{...}} placeholders remain unresolved. No error thrown for either handoff variable.

title: Concurrent handoff writes from parallel branches complete without race condition
description: When two parallel branches each write a different handoff simultaneously, the HandoffStore's per-ID write serializer must ensure both writes complete atomically and independently. A race condition would manifest as one write overwriting or corrupting the other, or as a file-not-found error from a torn write. This tests the concurrent write path that happens during every parallel step execution. Verify this specific multi-id concurrent scenario is not in handoffs.test.ts before implementing.
input: Create a HandoffStore pointing to a temp directory. Fire concurrently: Promise.all([ store.write('handoffA', { data: 'A', count: 1 }), store.write('handoffB', { data: 'B', count: 2 }) ]). Await both. Then read both back.
expected_output: Both writes complete without error. store.read('handoffA') returns ok({ data: 'A', count: 1 }). store.read('handoffB') returns ok({ data: 'B', count: 2 }). No cross-contamination between values. No AtomicWriteError or HandoffIoError.

title: hello-world flow completes end-to-end with step-1 handoff injected into step-2 via MockProvider
description: An end-to-end validation of the hello-world reference example using MockProvider (zero Claude API calls). This test exercises: defineFlow parsing, DAG construction, handoff write from step-1, context injection into step-2, and artifact write — the complete pipeline. If any part of the flow-authoring contract is broken, this test will catch it. It also serves as a living reference for flow authors.
input: Import the hello-world flow from examples/hello-world/flow.ts. Construct a MockProvider where: step 'greet' returns the string '{"greeting":"Hello Alice"}' and step 'summarize' returns the string '# Greeting\n\nHello Alice'. Run the flow with input { name: 'Alice' } and the MockProvider.
expected_output: run() resolves with status:'succeeded'. The handoff named 'greeting' in the run directory contains { greeting: 'Hello Alice' } (successfully parsed and stored). The artifact 'greeting.md' exists in the run's artifacts directory with content '# Greeting\n\nHello Alice'. No errors thrown.

title: codebase-discovery reference flow compiles to a valid Flow object (regression guard)
description: A regression guard for the canonical reference flow. Any change to the flow-compilation pipeline (defineFlow, buildGraph, step validators) that breaks the reference flow should be caught immediately. This test simply imports and validates the compiled output — it does not run any steps. It also validates the topological order to catch silent DAG changes.
input: Dynamically import the default export from packages/flows/codebase-discovery/flow.ts (or the compiled dist/flow.js). Inspect the returned object.
expected_output: The import succeeds without throwing. The default export is a valid Flow object: it has name:'codebase-discovery', a steps record with 4 entries (inventory, entities, services, report), and graph.topoOrder is an array of length 4. graph.topoOrder positions 'inventory' before 'entities', 'services', and 'report' (inventory must be first in topo order as the only root step).
